frames.description: the ground truth — what actually happened on the wreck-night and after
Halsa Crewe, the keeper's child, left to keep the Holm — the point of view
Bryde Crewe, the old keeper, abed with a broken leg
the revenue riding-officer, Crown, waiting for the cutter
Cass Pellow, the wreck's surviving mate
Ysolt Marran, the passenger with the locked sea-chest
Orne Veck, the Holm's headman and net-master
Dunna Quick, the fisher-widow first down at the ebb
Maon Skerry, the old pilot who once guided ships off the Sneck
Wick, the boy who found the first thing on the shore
Pell Garrow, the mainland factor's man, stranded by the tide
Senna, betrothed to the boy Wick

branches.description: Halsa lays it before the Crown and the revenue
Halsa keeps the Holm's part quiet; the wreck stands as the sea's
Halsa settles it on the Holm before the cutter comes

entities.description: the keeper's child, tending the light and tide-bell
the old keeper, Halsa's parent, abed with a broken leg
the revenue riding-officer
the wreck's surviving mate, Cass Pellow
Ysolt Marran, the passenger off the wreck
Orne Veck, the Holm's headman
Dunna Quick, the fisher-widow
Maon Skerry, the old pilot
Wick, the boy of the Holm
Pell Garrow, the factor's man
Eda Lay, the fish-curer
Senna, betrothed to Wick
the wreck's supercargo, the one dead man
the lantern that showed the false channel light from the Ness
the passenger's locked sea-chest
the one cask of cargo that went quietly up the Holm
the Holm's true light, doused on the wreck-night
the inheritance papers carried in the chest
the keeper's tally-book logging the lights and tides
the bill of lading the supercargo carried
the long shoal where ships go aground
the tidal causeway to the mainland
the Ness headland, where the false light was shown

predicates.description: who holds the object at this scene (one holder at a time)
the load-bearing wreck-night truth a subject is the cause of
where a load-bearing object stands, as a state that changes when it is found or moved
the state of a light — burning, out, or shown to have been doused by a hand

facts.claim: the Marran was lured onto the Sneck by a false channel light shown from the Ness headland, not lost to weather alone
Orne Veck the headman carried the lantern to the Ness and showed the false light to draw the cargo to the starving Holm
the Holm's true light was doused on the wreck-night so the false light would not be answered, and Orne Veck doused it
Bryde Crewe broke a leg on the light-stair that night climbing to relight the doused Holm light
the supercargo did not simply drown: Cass Pellow the mate held him under in the surf to take what he carried
the supercargo carried the bill of lading and the key to the sea-chest, and the mate took the bill from his body
the chest holds inheritance papers, a contested deed, not treasure; Ysolt Marran sailed to lodge it ashore
one cask of brandy off the wreck went quietly up the Holm, so the cargo ashore is one cask short of the bill of lading
Maon Skerry the old pilot did not show the false light, though he alone could read what a light on the Ness would do to a vessel in the channel
the new channel buoys took the pilot's living, which is why a light on the Ness could still bend a ship onto the Sneck
Halsa woke to the tide-bell unrung and the Holm light dark, and could not say why the light had failed
Orne knows the Holm light was dark when the Marran struck, and means no one else shall learn it was made dark
Bryde saw the Holm light go out and climbed the stair to relight it, and fell
by grey morning Halsa saw the shore strewn with the Marran's timber and the Drang already drowned, the Holm shut in
Eda the curer believes the Marran was an honest loss to the weather, as wrecks on the Sneck always are
the officer notes the Drang is under and no soul can leave the Holm until the long ebb bares it
Halsa took the keeper's tally to keep the Holm's account while Bryde lies abed
the tally's last line, in Bryde's hand, logs the Holm light OUT at the very hour the Marran struck
Bryde will not say from the bed how the light came to be out, only that Bryde did not douse it
Wick found a shuttered ship's lantern high on the bar at first light, dry of seawater, and took it up to the Holm
Wick first told it he found a drowned man, then a cask, then the lantern, and is afraid to say which is true
Senna knows Wick is lying about what he found, because he hid the true thing under the net-loft straw
Halsa heard the boy's three tellings and marked that something he found frightens him into lying
the officer set a tally of every cask and bale the sea brought up, to account to the Crown for the wreck
the officer does not yet know the count is short, taking the islanders' word for what came ashore
Pell Garrow the factor's man saw the casks counted and waits on the brandy his master is owed off the wreck
Cass Pellow tells the Holm he alone was washed living from the Marran and saw the supercargo go under in the dark
Cass keeps the dead man's bill of lading hidden, and means to claim the cargo as the wreck's only living officer
Halsa believed the mate's story of an honest drowning when first she heard it
Ysolt Marran will not say what is in her locked chest, only that it is hers and the mate must not have it
Ysolt knows the supercargo was her kin and carried the chest's key and the deed against her claim
Cass believes the chest holds coin or plate worth killing for, not knowing it holds only papers
Dunna Quick was first down at the ebb and carried one cask of brandy up to the Holm before the officer's count
Dunna knows the headman bid the Holm take what the sea gave and say nothing of it
Dunna believes the wreck a plain gift of the sea and does not know a false light drew it
Maon read the Marran's lie of timber on the bar and judged she had been steering to a light on the Ness, where no light belongs
Maon suspects the false light was shown but will not name a hand, having shown ships off the bar himself in the old days
Halsa heard the pilot say a ship had steered to a wrong light, and did not yet know whose
Orne tells the Holm to give the officer a plain wreck and a plain drowning, and to keep the Holm's own counsel
Orne believes the boy found only a body and does not know Wick took up the very lantern Orne carried to the Ness
Halsa marked that the headman is steering the whole Holm to one telling, and asked herself why
Pell Garrow holds his master's copy of the bill of lading and counts the brandy ashore one cask short of it
Pell suspects an islander has lifted a cask but takes the mate, not the Holm, for the thief
Cass lets the factor blame the islanders, the better to keep the lading-bill and the dead man's claim to himself
Halsa learned from Bryde that the Holm light was already dark when Bryde climbed the stair, so it did not fail of itself
Bryde tells Halsa the light was doused by a hand, not the weather, but did not see whose hand in the dark
Halsa now believes the dark Holm light was no accident, and that the wreck and the doused light are one matter
Wick, pressed by Halsa, owns at last that he found a dry shuttered lantern on the bar and hid it in the net-loft
Senna stood by Wick as he owned the lantern, and told Halsa where in the straw it lay
Halsa understood a dry lantern high on the bar could not have floated from a wreck, but was carried there by a hand
Halsa took the hidden lantern from the net-loft and saw it was Holm-work, not ship's gear, and had been burned dry
Halsa matched the short count of casks against the factor's bill and saw the Holm itself had taken from the wreck
Halsa pieced the doused light, the carried lantern and the false light on the Ness into one purpose against the Marran
with the Drang baring and the cutter due, Halsa must choose: REPORT to the Crown, SHELTER the Holm, or CONFRONT it on the shore
Halsa knows the false light and the short cask but does NOT yet know it was the mate who drowned the supercargo by hand
Orne sees the Drang baring and means to get the lantern back and the cargo away before the cutter can count it
Orne Veck holds the lantern, having carried it to the Ness on the wreck-night
the boy Wick holds the lantern, having found it on the bar and hidden it in the net-loft
Halsa holds the lantern, having taken it from the net-loft straw
Ysolt Marran holds the locked chest, brought living off the wreck
Dunna Quick holds the short cask of brandy, carried up before the officer's count
the officer holds the lantern, taken into Crown evidence and matched to the Ness
the officer holds the short cask, recovered from Dunna into the Crown's count
the officer holds the chest, taken into Crown keeping and broken open for evidence
Orne Veck holds the lantern again, taken back from Halsa and buried under the salt-house floor
the factor holds the short cask, set among the rest on the bar by Dunna's own hand
Halsa carried the lantern and the tally to the officer and named the doused light and the false light on the Ness
the officer, shown the dry lantern and the short count, believes the wreck was made and opens an inquiry on the Holm
the officer takes the locked chest into Crown keeping and breaks it open for evidence of the wreck
the officer found in the chest the contested deed, and saw why the supercargo was worth more dead than alive
Ysolt, her chest broken by the Crown, owns at last that the deed is hers and the supercargo died holding the key
Halsa, hearing of the deed, first guesses the supercargo's death was no honest drowning
Maon, called before the officer, owns that a light on the Ness would draw a ship to the Sneck, and that he did not show it
the officer takes the lantern into evidence and matches it to the Ness, and the false light is named to the Crown
Orne, faced with the lantern in the officer's hand, will not own the false light and lays the dark Holm light to the weather
Dunna, questioned by the officer, gives up the short cask rather than hang for the headman's wreck
Pell Garrow, his short cask recovered by the Crown, learns the thief was the Holm and not the mate he had blamed
Cass, seeing the inquiry close on the wreck-night, ran for the bared Drang at the ebb with the lading-bill
Wick, who first found the lantern, saw the mate run and raised the Holm to head him on the Drang
Halsa, finding the lading-bill on the caught mate, knew at last the supercargo had been drowned by a hand for what he carried
Senna kept the boy back from the running mate on the Drang, and the two of them held the net-loft against the tide
when the cutter came over the bared Drang the officer gave up the mate and the headman to the Crown's keeping
Halsa watched the cutter take the Holm's own headman away, and knew the law had it now, not she
Maon, cleared of the false light by his own word, stayed to pilot the cutter back over the Sneck at the ebb
Eda the curer, who had believed it an honest loss, salted the wreck's spoiled fish and grieved the headman taken
Bryde, mending abed, heard the cutter sail and was glad the doused light was owned to the Crown, not buried
Ysolt left on the cutter with her deed lodged and the supercargo's death named, her claim safe and her kin a murderer's victim
Pell Garrow saw his master's cargo restored to the Crown's tally and left on the cutter with the count made whole
Dunna kept her neck but not her name, marked on the Holm as the one who gave the headman up to the Crown
Halsa kept the light and the tally true, and the Holm lived on shut and shamed, its headman gone to the Crown's law
Halsa chose to let the wreck stand as the sea's accident, and gave the officer the plain drowning the headman bid
the officer, given a plain wreck and an honest drowning, wrote the Marran a loss to the weather and the Sneck
Bryde, told by Halsa the light would be laid to the weather, was grieved but kept the bed and the silence
Orne took the lantern back from Halsa and buried it under the salt-house floor, the false light gone for good
Halsa let the headman take the lantern, holding the Holm's whole life worth more than one drowned stranger's truth
Maon knew the false light was buried, not undone, and said nothing, an old wrecker holding an old silence
Ysolt, the wreck named the sea's, bargained with the headman to keep her chest unopened in return for her silence
Halsa let the passenger keep her chest closed, the deed unread, the supercargo's death left an honest drowning
Cass, the wreck laid to the weather, kept the lading-bill and the dead man's claim, his hand never named
Wick, sick with the lantern's secret, near told the officer the truth before the cutter came
Senna caught Wick at the officer's door and held him from telling, to keep the Holm and Wick from the rope
Halsa took the boy from the officer's door and bound him to the Holm's silence, the secret now the children's too
Dunna kept the short cask hidden and the silence whole, and the count was never made right to the factor
when the cutter came the officer carried off only a plain wreck's report, suspecting nothing of the false light
Pell Garrow left on the cutter with his count still short, sure an islander had robbed him and unable to prove it
Halsa watched the cutter sail with the truth still on the Holm, and the Holm whole, and herself no longer clean of it
Orne, the cutter gone, ruled the Holm as before, but the whole hamlet now held his secret over him
Wick grew quiet and apart, the lantern's truth a stone in him, no longer the boy who told it three ways
Ysolt stayed on the Holm past the cutter, her chest and her claim unsettled, bound to the island by the bargain
Eda the curer, still believing the wreck the sea's honest work, wondered only why the Holm had grown so quiet
Bryde, mending, kept a true tally in secret of the doused light, in case the Holm should ever need the truth
Halsa kept the Holm whole and the light true, and carried the wreck-night's silence as the price of the island's life
Halsa called the whole Holm down to the shore at the ebb to name the wreck-night before the cutter could come
Orne came to the shore meaning to put down the talk, not knowing Halsa held the very lantern he had carried
Maon stood with Halsa on the bar and told the gathered Holm what a light on the Ness does to a ship in the channel
Halsa held up the dry Holm-work lantern before the Holm and named it the false light shown from the Ness
Wick told the gathered Holm to its face that he found the dry lantern on the bar, his three tellings ended
Orne, the lantern named before the Holm, owned at last that he showed the false light to bring the cargo to a starving island
Senna stood with Wick before the Holm, that he should not bear the telling alone
Ysolt, the wreck owned a made thing, opened her own chest on the bar and showed the deed, to name why the supercargo died
Halsa, shown the deed, charged the mate before the Holm with drowning the supercargo by hand for what he carried
Cass, the deed shown and the Holm against him, broke and owned he held the supercargo under to take the lading-bill
Pell Garrow, hearing the mate own the killing, knew his short cask the least wrong done on the wreck-night
Dunna brought the short cask down to the bar and set it among the rest, the count made whole by the Holm's own hand
Halsa held the reckoning on the bar: the headman's wreck and the mate's killing owned by the Holm, not the Crown
Orne gave himself to the Holm's judgment and to whatever the cutter would make of a confessed wrecker
Maon, the false light named and owned, was cleared on the bar before the whole Holm, his old silence at an end
Bryde, carried to the shore on a hurdle, told the Holm the doused light had broken the keeper's own leg
the officer, come down to the shore, found the Holm had judged its own and held the mate and headman for the cutter
Halsa answered the cutter for the whole Holm, giving up the mate and the headman with the truth told first by the island
Ysolt left on the cutter with her deed shown and her kin's death named on the Holm, not wrung out by the Crown
Pell Garrow left with his count made whole by the Holm's own hand and a strange respect for the island that judged itself
Eda the curer, who had thought it the sea's honest work, saw the Holm name its own guilt and never again called it accident
Wick, who told it true at the last, kept the bar with Senna as the cutter took the headman and the mate away
Halsa kept the Holm whole by naming its wrong herself, and the island lived on having judged its own before the law could
